data.title: Diversification Is Dying: 2 Big Risks About To Reshape The Market
data.description:
Written by Leo Nelissen and Albert Marko

Introduction

I have used the words “regime change” quite a bit in recent years. As strong as they may sound, they were fully warranted, as I have these, like the long-term rotation from growth to value. While it took a while to unfold, 2026 was the year that the market found out that the software trade wasn’t sustainable anymore and that hard-asset companies may be the place to be for two big reasons:

  • They are hard to disrupt.
  • They benefit from economic growth broadening, which is another thesis of mine.
  • They tend to have strong pricing power (inflation protection).

That’s the regime change I care so much about, as I often say, that, as important as stock picking may be, getting the Big Picture right is even more important.

Today, we'll break down two major market risks that we expect to turn into tailwinds for investors who play their cards right and potentially end up on the “receiving end” of a massive fund rotation.

So, let’s get to it!

Risk 1 - The End of Easy 60/40 Gains

The 60/40 portfolio may be one of the most well-known strategies besides the simple set-and-forget investments in a 100% equity fund. That makes sense, as a 60% stocks and 40% bonds breakdown provides both growth and safety.

During strong years, stocks outperform bonds because of elevated earnings growth, dividends, and potential multiple expansion. During bad years, bonds tend to rise as central banks intervene, capital moves into safe places, and similar reasons.

The iShares Core 60/40 Balanced Allocation ETF (AOR) above is a great example of this. For example, between 2009 and 2021, both bonds and stocks did well due to factors like low inflation, globalization, central bank stimulus, and others. Then, after 2021, a poor bond performance was more than offset by a strong stock market.

Now, that's changing, as even asset manager KKR calls this a regime change:

Since 2020, we have argued that four forces are reshaping the macro backdrop: Larger fiscal deficits, heightened geopolitical uncertainty, a messy energy transition, and stickier, more volatile inflation. Against this backdrop, we are seeing the historical relationship between stocks and bonds become less reliable, as both become more positively correlated with each other. Said another way, in this Regime Change environment, stocks and bonds can be under pressure simultaneously, which can render the traditional 60/40 framework less effective in delivering the same level of diversification benefits, particularly during periods of macro and geopolitical uncertainty. - KKR

As the quote shows, KKR highlights something we have often brought up, which is that because of factors like elevated deficits, political uncertainty, and sticky inflation, the favorable relationship between stocks and bonds is breaking down.

The chart below shows this quite well:

Suddenly, bonds aren’t the safe haven they used to be, which is why we expect that many investors, especially large institutional ones that have bought 60/40 assets on autopilot, may soon be forced to make changes.

At Main Street Alpha, we’re laser-focused on these developments, as we know that the places where capital may be forced to go could be like the pot of gold at the end of a rainbow (except they aren’t fake).

But first, there’s a second risk we need to go over.

Risk 2 - The “Hidden” Commodity Risk

Commodities are another major asset class in addition to bonds and stocks.

This area includes energy commodities, grains, oilseeds, livestock, industrial metals, precious metals, and niche products.

Back in the early 2000s, commodities became popular among investors. However, they weren’t randomly buying commodities but actually adding economic value. For example, if a farmer wanted to sell crops forward (using futures) to lock in a fixed price (hedging his output), institutions often became the counterparty. The farmer went short, while a fund went long.

This provided liquidity for the farmer. Also, these funds benefited from “roll yield,” which basically means that in backwardation, their discounted futures price would eventually converge with higher spot prices.

Again, this was very smart, and it worked for both parties.

That has changed, as commodities have become very popular in recent years, especially as inflation protection tools. When prices rise, you may as well own the commodities. Unfortunately, because so much money went into commodities, it created a contango, which is the exact opposite of backwardation. Now, funds were forced to sell cheap expiring contracts and roll them over into more expensive ones.

Also, the economic benefit is largely gone, as these trading volumes tend to be way higher than what producers require to hedge. It also means that many key commodity funds are poor long-term investments, as rolling over in contango is expensive and can hurt a fund’s performance.

The United States Oil Fund (USO) is a good example of this. It’s one of the most popular oil funds that has done very poorly on a long-term basis, mostly because of fees. That risk is getting way worse in times of inflation when even more (institutional) capital is flooding into these areas.

And to bring up problem No. 1 again, commodity funds may not be the best way to diversify if funds make changes to their 60/40 approach.

That’s exactly why these risks are so important.

Some Solutions to Keep in Mind

This is Seeking Alpha. Odds are you are already in a good spot, as this is a website for active investors, or at least those who make their own decisions instead of piling their cash into 60/40 or similar strategies.

It’s not based on data (that I don’t have), but I think it’s a fair assumption.

So, to leave you with some food for thought, some of our favorite ideas are the ones that could be on the receiving end of these cash flows. In the case of the 60/40 risk, we like a mix of short-term T-bills and cash equivalents with some higher-risk private credit.

Although private credit is in no way, shape, or form a substitute for risk-free government bonds, if we combine higher-yielding short-term debt (that benefits from the Fed’s inability to aggressively cut in a high-inflation environment) with private credit that has both income and growth (think of Ares Management (ARES) that yields close to 5%), we can create a solution that provides income, a great risk/reward, and offsetting factors to the 60/40 correlation risk.

If you require a higher yield, picks like Capital Southwest (CSWC) may be more suited.

Then, in the commodity space, we believe that equities will gain tremendous popularity in the years ahead. Unlike commodity ETFs, corporations aren’t financial vehicles. That’s one of the reasons I have always preferred to buy producers instead of the commodities they extract from the ground or grow in a field.

Also, unlike commodity ETFs, these companies can pay dividends and buy back stock.

  • For example, Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) yields close to 4% and has hiked its dividend for 25 consecutive years. I prefer it over an oil ETF any day. The same applies to other companies like Exxon Mobil (XOM), Chevron (CVX), and EOG Resources (EOG). I expect energy equities, in general, to gain a lot of popularity for years to come, especially due to years of underperformance. If these continue to rotate, I think there’s massive alpha here.

  • In agriculture, I prefer companies that produce fertilizer like Nutrien (NTR), Mosaic (MOS), CF Industries (CF), and diversified processors and traders like Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) and Bunge (BG). Generally speaking, when agricultural prices rebound, these see higher margins. When combined with their high volumes, they tend to make for great cyclical value trades. However, on a long-term basis, they need to be handled with extreme care due to volatility.

Moreover, I would add midstream and infrastructure stocks like Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) that offer income, exposure to energy without direct commodity price risk, and consistent shareholder value. These, too, are great substitutes for 60/40 portfolios, as they also incorporate the commodity aspect.

In other words, I would maintain a balanced barbell strategy where the core consists of safe and consistent compounders (like an S&P 500 ETF), with additional exposure to commodity equities, some agriculture, midstream, and similar investments that position us at what I expect to be the receiving end of a long-term rotation among institutional investors.

Takeaway

I’m convinced we’re in a regime change where traditional diversification is breaking down, and passive strategies may not work the way they were intended.

This includes 60/40 risks and changes in the commodity space that most didn’t foresee a few years ago, especially not in light of inflation risks.

To me, however, that’s not just a warning but mostly an opportunity, as I believe capital will rotate into a new home, especially among institutional investors. That makes me even more bullish on hard-asset equities, income-generating vehicles, and smart credit strategies.

While not all of these are for the faint of heart, combining them with a strong core of, i.e., S&P 500 exposure, may be a great way to build wealth and even alpha versus the S&P 500 and 60/40 alternatives.

data.title: Hold Fairly Valued Deutsche Telekom Shares As An Investment In U.S. Telecoms
data.description:
Introduction: Deutsche Telekom In The U.S. Wireless Major League Since Five Years Through T-Mobile U.S.

When I last wrote an article about Deutsche Telekom (DTEGF)(DTEGY), titled "The Case For Deutsche Telekom Next To AT&T and Verizon", it was the start of 2021 and the merger between Sprint and T-mobile US (TMUS) had just concluded. Deutsche Telekom had obtained actual majority ownership of the merged U.S. entity through shares and call options. There was plenty to write about then, because the Sprint-T-mobile merger had significant consequences for both Deutsche Telekom ánd the U.S. wireless competitive landscape.

As an introduction, I'd like to quickly recap the impact of the events from five years ago, because it's still very relevant context for this article. In my opinion, Deutsche Telekom's business can't be properly understood without going into the Sprint - T-mobile U.S. merger.

Prior to the merger, the Deutsche Telekom's business was equally split over the U.S. and Germany/Europe with revenue from both regions being just over $40B. The merger with Sprint resulted in an enlarged T-mobile U.S. and this meant that Deutsche Telekom, as the owner, became primarily a U.S. telco. A major part of the total company revenue was suddenly U.S. generated. The German home market and some other European countries, jointly generated only a smaller part of the total revenue. The image below with the revenue split per region in 2025 illustrates the importance of the U.S. market for Deutsche Telekom. It shows that $90.3B in revenue of the group total of 120.9B euro and $73.3B of the total 101.1B euro service revenue came from the U.S. So, from a revenue perspective, Deutsche Telekom is now primarily an American company with roots in Germany.

The merger with Sprint also had a major impact on the competitive landscape in wireless communications in the U.S.: the post-merger T-mobile U.S. served ~140M subscribers in total, which included the second largest postpaid subscriber base in the U.S. This put Deutsche Telekom in the same league as AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ). Until then, wireless had been a "rigid oligopoly" with AT&T and Verizon as the two market leaders. This changed irreversibly from then on. As an illustration, in this report from 2023, two years after the merger, T-mobile U.S. is named the leading U.S. wireless operator with the fastest 4G network in no less than 46 of 50 states and a strong position in 5G as well. In this update, from another two years later in 2025, it becomes clear that T-mobile U.S.' subscriber growth in wireless is consistently outpacing the competition, leaving AT&T and VZ to deal with the fallout. It's clear that the merger from five years ago meant a tectonic shift in the competitive dynamics in the wireless U.S. market that still reverberates today.

With the above introduction in mind, this article now revisits Deutsche Telekom. We'll take a look at how the business is evolving and what the prospects are for investors here. The Deutsche Telekom FY2025 results are already available, so these provide fresh input.

To immediately summarize the key finding in this article: the fate of any investments in Deutsche Telekom is overwhelmingly dependent on the continued success of T-mobile U.S., while the German and European markets have only a modest role to play at best.

Investment Thesis: Only Add Deutsche Telekom Shares To The Telecoms Portfolio At The Right Price

A straightforward investment thesis for buying shares in a mature telco is that the share ownership brings income through dividends and capital gains from rising share prices. This applies to investing in both Deutsche Telekom and many other telcos in the major league, be it Vodafone (VOD), Telefónica (TEF), AT&T or Verizon. Telecoms has been no high growth industry since the dotcom era, but the telcos are reliable dividend payers and they generally still grow slowly. If shares are bought at the right time when prices are low, the slow growth can gradually drive share prices up, adding to the return from dividends.

Logically, the two investment goals with investing in Deutsche Telekom, i.e. getting dividend payouts plus the returns from capital gains, are best achieved when shares are bought at the lowest price possible. Buying shares at low prices locks in a relatively high dividend yields and optimizes the chance of capital gains from rising share prices later on.

While the above thesis is straightforward, it's not so easy, in practice, to determine when telco shares are attractively priced. When are the shares "as cheap as possible?". It's key to determine this, because a badly timed purchase at high share prices may result in share prices going down afterwards. Investor may then be looking at unrealized capital losses and, in bad cases, these may exist on paper for a long time and negate the dividends that are received. This raises the question what the valuation of Deutsche Telekom currently looks like and if the shares are cheap or expensive.

Deutsche Telekom Valuation: A Double Over The Last Five Years With T-mobile U.S. As The Main Driver

The graph below shows the Deutsche Telekom share price over the last five years. It shows nearly a double from just under $20 to just under $40 over the period. Note that this is the ADR's price graph, closely mirroring the European shares graph. The European shares trade on the German exchange "Xetra" in Frankfurt.

The Deutsche Telekom sharecount hasn't changed much over the last five years, continuously hovering around the 4.9B shares, so the rising share price represents an equal increase in market cap of the company. The Deutsche Telekom market cap rose by ~64B euro (=~$74B) to reach the current ~150B euro.

If there's any other remark that needs to be added with the graph it's that net debt (including leases) at Deutsche Telekom has increased by ~12B euro over the last five years from 120B to 132.5B euro. Deutsche Telekom can easily handle this increased debt with the growing revenue and EBITDA and an investment grade credit rating, but if debt hadn't risen by this amount, the shares could have gained even more, assuming a similar enterprise value.

Deutsche Telekom reported a 1.97 euro Earnings Per Share ("EPS") over 2025. At the start of 2026, this meant the P/E ratio was 14x when the shares traded at 28 euro but this rose to just over 15x with the shares at ~31 euro at the time of writing this. To put the valuation into perspective, we can compare it with the P/E ratio of the two U.S. peers: AT&T and VZ. These are trading at lower multiples than Deutsche Telekom with P/E ratios in the 10 - 12x range recently, so Deutsche Telekom is commanding a premium.

In the introduction at the start if this article, the point was made that Deutsche Telekom can't be viewed separately from subsidiary T-mobile U.S., that is now responsible for roughly two-thirds of the total Deutsche Telekom revenue. So, what's the influence that T-mobile U.S. had on the Deutsche Telekom's rising share price and valuation over the last five years?

As the above graph shows, T-mobile U.S. shares have seen a significant run-up over the last five years since the merger with Sprint, just like the Deutsche Telekom shares. They're currently trading hands at ~$190, while at the end of 2020, the shares traded at a much lower ~$135.

Deutsche Telekom owns a gradually growing 52.6% of the T-mobile U.S. shares per the beginning of 2026 (see image below) and the run up of the T-mobile U.S. share price means that this ownership becomes ever more valuable over time, driving an increased Deutsche Telekom valuation.

To put numbers on how much the rising value of the TMUS shares helped Deutsche Telekom's stake we can have a look at the T-mobile U.S. market cap five years ago and now.

Deutsche Telekom TMUS stake value TMUS Total Shares Outstanding TMUS Share Price ($) TMUS Market Cap ($) Value TMUS stake (stake size)
Q1 2021 1.154B 135 155.9B ~78B (50%)
Q1 2026 1.195B 201 240.4B ~127B (52.6%)

The above table shows that the T-mobile U.S. market cap rose from $155.9B to $240.4B for an increase of $84.5B over the last five years. This means that the Deutsche Telekom stake of 52.6% per YE2025 has gained ~$49B (=127 - 78) in value over the period. We saw above that Deutsche Telekom's market cap rose 64B euro over the last five years. $49B of this is thus directly attributable to the increased value of the ownership of the TMUS stake.

Next to the benefit from the rising value of the share ownership, Deutsche Telekom consolidates the T-mobile U.S. results and that's why I'd argue that T-Mobile U.S. performance is responsible for virtually all of the increased value of Deutsche Telekom over the last five years. The revenue and free cash flow growth of the entire Deutsche Telekom group is almost all due to the T-mobile U.S. growth. Let's look at the top line numbers as an example. Deutsche Telekom grew the group top line from ~101B euro in 2020 to ~121B euro in 2025. T-mobile U.S. was responsible for more than 80% of that higher revenue with it's top line growing from ~$68B in 2020 to ~$88B in 2025 for an increase of ~$20B. The same applies to EBITDA and Free Cash Flow growth. T-Mobile U.S. is responsible for over 80% of growth of Deutsche Telekom in these finanical metrics as well. The non U.S. regions where Deutsche Telekom is active hardly show any growth that exceeds inflation. The U.S. market is thus clearly the growth engine for the entire Deutsche Telekom group and, as such, the driver for the higher valuation.

To finalize this review of the valuation, it may be useful to note that T-mobile U.S. had $9.72 EPS in 2025. This means the P/E ratio of T-mobile U.S. stands at over ~19x at the time of writing, which is a step up from the Deutsche Telekom P/E ratio of 15x and almost double the valuation of AT&T and VZ. The EPS is still growing, so the forward P/E ratio is slightly lower at ~17x, but still significantly higher than the P/E ratio of the peers. In the next section, we can see that the T-mobile U.S. growth numbers may well justify that TMUS shares have this higher valuation, but I'd also like to point out that Deutsche Telekom's most important subsidiary in the U.S. is no longer cheap with the P/E ratio of ~19x.

There's a few takeaways from this section. The first important one is that Deutsche Telekom has seen rising share prices over the last five years and is now trading at a premium to the peers AT&T and Verizon. The second one is that T-mobile U.S. is clearly the driving force behind Deutsche Telekom's value increase over the last few years by singlehandedly driving over 80% of revenue, EBITDA and free cash flow growth. Third, Deutsche Telekom owns over 52% of TMUS shares that significantly rose in price, making the ownership of the stake much more valuable, adding $49B over a five year period. Finally, T-Mobile U.S. as the most important Deutsche Telekom subsidiary is trading at an even higher premium in comparison with peers than the parent.

The thesis at the start of this section stated that it's the challenge to buy Deutsche Telekom shares as cheaply as possible. With the Deutsche Telekom shares trading at a premium in comparison with peers AT&T and Verizon, one can and should question if the shares are "as cheap as possible". The T-mobile U.S. subsidiary shares are trading at en even steeper premium, so the same question applies there. We'll need to have a look at the prospects of the Deutsche Telekom and the U.S. subsidiary to see if the outlook supports the premiums or if the shares are getting expensive here.

Deutsche Telekom Prospects Justify Current Premium Valuation Over Peers, But Shares Now Fairly Valued

Now take a look at the below outlook for 2026 and 2027 from the 2025 earnings presentation:

The two columns on the right-hand of the image show the growth expectations for 2026 and 2027 per region. U.S. revenue is expected to "increase", while Germany and Europe will only show a "slight increase". One can read "slight increase" as low single-digit and "increase" as high single-digit.

From the image it becomes clear that T-mobile U.S. is set to continue to be the growth engine for Deutsche Telekom like in the last five year. Germany and Europe will remain less important contributors to growth. System Solutions, the technology delivery part of the Deutsche Telekom group, will also grow healthily, with an "increase", but this part of the group is too small in size to have any material impact.

At the bottom of the above image, there's the EBITDA numbers per region for 2025 and the expected EBITDA numbers for 2026. These confirm that the U.S. market will drive the bulk of the growth again with an EBITDA rise from $33.5B to $36.2B in that region. Germany and Europe are only expected to jointly deliver an EBITDA growth of less than 0.5B euro in 2026 in comparison with 2025.

So, what is happening in the German home market? the above image says that Germany will deliver a "slight increase" in 2026. When we take into account the annual inflation of just over 2% in 2025 and 2026, the German home market is, in all fairness, a stagnant market. Expected revenue and EBIRDA growth will only just exceeds this 2% inflation. Note that inflation in Germany was much higher than 2% in the years before 2025, reaching 5% or more directly after the Covid pandemic. This high inflation has negatively offset the Deutsche Telekom performance in Germany.

Deutsche Telekom is rolling out fiber in the home market. Fiber is the key area of investments by far. While this drives some broadband adds in Germany (+164k households), this is not nearly enough growth in real terms to make the home market as important as the U.S. market. One explanation why it's a challenge to grow in Germany is that the German telecoms market is a tough market with many operators, inclusing satellite and cable companies, competing aggressively for the households to sign up.

Things are only slightly more favorable in the other European markets where Deutsche Telekom is active, like Romania. While growth there is a little faster that in Germany with a 3.9% year-on-year service revenue growth, the size of this part of the business is too small to make much of difference in the grand scheme of things.

This brings us again to the U.S., where the service revenue is set to keep growing by between 5 and 10% annually in the next two years. The service revenue from postpaid subscriptions is one area of growth as the next trend overview shows:

Postpaid subscribers are high value in comparison with prepaid, so a positive growth trend for this subscription type is crucial for a good overall financial performance. It's good news that the year-on-year quarterly postpaid service revenue growth reached a recent record of 10.5% in Q4 2025.

The other product that helps growth in the U.S. is broadband. Fiber to the home broadband offerings are an important growth area for T-mobile U.S. with subcribing households set to grow from 8.5M households now to 15M households in 2030. While this growth is more than healthy, wireless postpaid remains the most important product by far with well over 100M subscribers. T-mobile U.S. management forecasts growth to continue for the postpaid service, but at a slower pace in 2026. 2025 saw 1.2M subscriber adds, while the forecast is 1M adds in 2026.

We saw that the T-mobile U.S. valuation with a P/E-ratio of ~19x is much higher than the valuation of peers AT&T and VZ. AT&T and Verizon have both shared a 2026 outlook with a low single-digit overall revenue growth. T-mobile U.S. is expected to grow much faster, so a higher valuation makes sense.

All in all, the prospect for Deutsche Telekom is, at least for the upcoming two years, that T-mobile U.S. will remain the growth driver, while the German home market and European markets will at best have a growth that slightly exceeds inflation. Deutsche Telekom will overall grow with a rate that is in between the two. Given these prospects for the different regions, it makes sense that Deutsche Telekom shares are trading at a premium in comparison with AT&T and Verizon, and that TMUS shares are trading at an even higher premium, which is why I'd conclude that the Deutsche Telekom shares look fairly valued.

Deutsche Telekom Dividend Is Ok, But Peers Offer Higher Yields

The premium valuations of Deutsche Telekom and T-mobile U.S. in comparison with AT&T and Verizon have the effect that the dividend yield on Deutsche Telekom or T-mobile U.S. shares is relatively modest. In my opinion. this again makes the shares in Deutsche Telekom a "hold' rather than a buy. Deutsche Telekom and T-mobile U.S. currently offer a yield at the lower end of the peer group (2 - 3 % gross), AT&T offers a near 4% yield and VZ an over 5% yield. What is worth mentioning here is that T-mobile U.S. only started paying dividends in 2023, currently around 2%, which is only slightly lower than the 3% dividend by the parent Deutsche Telekom.

Risks

For holders of Deutsche Telekom shares, I'd see any risks of increased competition in the U.S market as key, given the importance of this market in terms of size and growh. Competition may be increasing for T-mobile U.S. going forward, which would have an immediate negative effect on Deutsche Telekom overall performance.

One example risk in the wireless space is the potential impact from the upcoming merger between Charter (CHTR) and Cox Communications (privately owned). This combination will have well over ~10M mobile subscribers. Charter is currently claiming to be the fastest growing U.S. mobile operator with 2 million subscriber adds over 2025. Aggressive marketing from this newly formed player with new mobile propositions can have significant impact for the established players. The Charter-Cox merger has yet to close, so for 2026, I'd expect them to need some time to gain speed in joint marketing, but there could be intensifying competition in 2027 and onward.

In the fixed-line space, I'd like to mention the AT&T plans to accelerate investments in fiber, posing a threat to T-mobile. U.S. ambitions for broadband expansion. Finally, for the rural areas, I'd like to mention the increasing importance of satellite broadband, as provided by, for example, Starlink (STRLK). This technology may have lower up- and download speeds than in fiber, but may also come at lower subscription prices. It's relatively early days for this type of satellite services, so it's an unknow what the size of the effect of this access technology will be, but it's competition nonetheless.

Outside competitive risks, the internal financial risks with investing in Deutsche Telekom are low. Deutsche Telekom had 98.1B euro net debt (excl. leases) per end of 2025 with a leverage of 2.5x and an investment grade credit rating. There's no reason for investors to avoid investing due to any distress.

Conclusion And Investor Takeaway

The title of this article states that holding Deutsche Telekom shares is investing in U.S. Telecoms. This is because two-thirds of the business is in the U.S. nowadays, so we're not talking about a primarily German telco anymore. The U.S. market is also where the growth is for Deutsche Telekom going forward, so the home market will have an even smaller role to play in the coming years.

Investors who chose, five years ago, to diversify their telecoms holding to include Deutsche Telekom shares have little cause to complain. The shares have roughly doubled in price as we saw above. T-mobile U.S. has proven to be a true growth engine for the Deutsche Telekom group.

Deutsche Telekom and the biggest subsidiary T-mobile U.S. are trading at a premium in comparison with peers. This makes sense as they're set to keep grow faster than the competition. The Deutsche Telekom shares now look fairly valued, though, and the dividend yield is at the lower end of what is available from the peer group, so I'd rate the shares a hold. Increasing competition in the U.S. may well drive Deutsche Telekom shares lower at some point in the future, potentially presenting an opportunity to buy the shares at lower prices.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tamboran Resources: Well Cost Reductions Play A Key Role In Unlocking Beetaloo Basin
data.description:
Tamboran Resources (TBN) is focused on exploring and commercializing natural gas production in Australia's Beetaloo Basin. The Beetaloo Basin is seen as a key future source of natural gas for Australia, although exploration/appraisal has been going on for a while and the prior main owner sold its assets there to Tamboran several years ago to focus more on green energy. The recent turmoil in the energy markets has probably further entrenched natural gas in Australia's future energy portfolio, though.

Tamboran's largest shareholder is Bryan Sheffield (formerly of Parsley Energy), who owned approximately 18% of Tamboran's shares in September 2025. Tamboran's CEO was formerly COO of Seneca Resources (an Appalachian Basin producer with around 1.2 Bcf per day of net production). It views the Beetaloo Basin as being similar to the early days (late 2000s) of U.S. shale development and is aiming for similar substantial gains in well productivity and cost improvements over time.

The premium pricing for Australian natural gas means that capital efficiency doesn't need to be similar to key U.S. natural gas plays such as the Haynesville and Marcellus. However, I believe Tamboran likely needs to get at least close to its $15 million longer-term well cost target for a 10,000-foot lateral (down from around $26 million with its recent well) to make the well economics quite solid.

If things work out well for Tamboran, it could be worth over $150 per share by 2032. However, the significant risks in terms of exploring the Beetaloo Basin, increasing production and well productivity, reducing well costs, building out infrastructure, and funding all lead me to estimate its current value at around $41 per share.

Tamboran's Fiscal Year ends on June 30. I will mention FY when referring to fiscal year, but the default will be calendar year.

Tamboran's primary listing is on the NYSE, but it also has CDIs (CHESS Depository Interests) that trade on the ASX in Australian dollars. One CDI represents 1/200th of a common share. This report also uses U.S. dollars as the main currency.

Beetaloo Basin

The Beetaloo Basin (also sometimes referred to as the Beetaloo sub-basin within the larger McArthur Basin) is located in Australia's Northern Territory. The Northern Territory is sparsely populated (under 300,000 inhabitants in an area nearly twice the size of Texas). The Beetaloo Basin is located around 300 miles away from the main city (Darwin) in the Northern Territory.

The Beetaloo Basin was previously undeveloped, but some estimates mention that there could be over 500 Tcf in natural gas resources there (although realistically only a fraction of that is ever likely to be recovered), along with the potential for near 6 Bcf per day in production.

The Beetaloo Basin is thus seen as very important to both the local natural gas supply and supplying Australia's LNG export terminals.

There are significant challenges, though, including the remoteness of the area, the need to build out infrastructure in such a remote area, and the technical issues around figuring out how to properly develop a new play.

Tamboran is the largest player in the Beetaloo Basin, with a position of around 2.9 million net acres and over 40,000 potential gross locations after its recent Falcon acquisition. This acquisition is expected to close soon, although the deadline was pushed back a bit as they work through some regulatory items.

Future Plans

Tamboran's plan is to initially supply the local Northern Territory natural gas market. This is a relatively small market, though (with average demand under 0.1 Bcf per day), so after that it will expand to supplying Australia's East Coast market. This market is much larger (at around 1 Bcf per day domestically), plus Tamboran can also help supply the Queensland LNG terminals (with export capacity that is several times more than domestic demand).

Longer-term, Tamboran is contemplating developing the integrated NTLNG export project. It previously mentioned a 2030 timeline for that project, although I believe it is likely to enter service at least a couple of years after that if it does go ahead.

Premium Natural Gas Pricing

Tamboran's natural gas production is expected to realize premium prices. It notes that the contracted price for 2027 supply to Australia's East Coast is approximately US$9.20 per Mcf, well above Henry Hub pricing (with my long-term expectation for Henry Hub being around US$3.75).

While there will be significant transportation costs (an older presentation mentioned initial costs of around US$2.70 per Mcf to access the East Coast markets before a new pipeline is built), margins should still be quite healthy due to the strong natural gas prices.

Australia's natural gas markets are heavily tied to LNG exports, with around 70% to 80% of Australia's natural gas production going to LNG exports.

Well Productivity and Costs

Tamboran mentions that the Shenandoah South pilot area wells have delivered early results in line with Marcellus wells. However, it should be noted that the data set includes a mix of older and newer wells, and newer Marcellus wells typically have considerably stronger performance than the older ones.

For example, Chesapeake Energy (as it was named at the time) mentioned an average IP30 of 26 MMCFE per day for its 2022 Marcellus wells (although those wells had an average length of close to 11,000 feet). The D&C costs for those Marcellus wells were under $1,000 per lateral foot. Those were relatively strong results, but I think an IP30 in the 15 to 20 MMCFE per day range (for a 10,000-foot well) would be reasonable to expect from an average newer Marcellus well.

Tamboran mentions recent costs around $26 million per well but is aiming to drive this down to $15 million by 2030 for a 10,000-foot lateral. This would still be well above the sub-$10 million cost for Marcellus wells, but Tamboran's operating margins per Mcf could be around double that of Marcellus wells due to the premium natural gas pricing.

Thus, if Tamboran can actually get well costs down to $15 million, a modest improvement in well productivity (from Shenandoah South 6H levels) should be pretty competitive in terms of economics.

Increasing Share Count

Tamboran has been working towards proving out its Beetaloo Basin position and has incurred significant exploration costs over the years while generating no material revenues yet. For example, it reported $137 million in incurred exploration costs in FY2025 and $76 million in incurred exploration costs in FY2024.

This has resulted in substantial cash needs, which can be seen from the following list of Tamboran's equity raises from July 2023 to June 2025. Tamboran's share count increased from 7.1 million at the start of July 2023 to 16.7 million at the end of June 2025.

This continued during the second half of 2025, with Tamboran's share count increasing to 21.1 million by the end of 2025.

As of the start of February 2026, Tamboran had 22.6 million common shares outstanding. The Falcon acquisition will add another 6.5 million shares, and its recent public offering and entitlement offers could add another 5.7 million shares.

Thus Tamboran's outstanding share count would reach around 35 million from the above items alone.

Cash Position

Tamboran mentioned that it had $138 million in pro forma cash as of early April 2026. This doesn't include the effect of cash burn during the first few months of 2026 but does include the impact of various other items, such as a recent PIPE transaction.

It aimed to raise $198 million from the public offering and entitlement offers, which should put its pro forma cash position around $330 million after factoring in offering expenses.

Tamboran used $81 million in cash for operating and investing (such as capex) activities during the second half of 2025. At that pace it would have enough cash until around early 2028 (although it wouldn't wait until its cash balance was near zero before raising more money). Tamboran is aiming to start commercial sales of natural gas later in 2026 but isn't expected to reach positive free cash flow for at least a few years.

Notes on Valuation

Tamboran is a very speculative company, with a large amount of potential, but also significant work that needs to be done to unlock that potential.

I will outline a successful scenario for Tamboran below.

Based on Australian natural gas pricing, Tamboran's EBITDA margins could end up around US$4 to US$5 per Mcf, around double that of my longer-term expectations for dry Marcellus production.

If it can ramp up net production to around 0.8 Bcf per day by 2032 (a couple of years later than its previously stated goal), that would translate into approximately $1.3 billion per year in EBITDA for Tamboran.

I believe that Tamboran could potentially maintain that production with around $600 million to $700 million in capex once its base decline moderates if it can also successfully reduce its well costs. That would leave it with around $0.6 to $0.7 billion per year in free cash flow (before income taxes), although it is expected to keep spending on production growth beyond that.

I'd value Tamboran at around $7.8 billion in 2032 in that scenario, which would be around an 8% free cash flow yield or a 6.0x EBITDA multiple. The relatively high valuation would be supported by Tamboran's large resource base and future growth potential.

I am also modeling Tamboran's future share count at 50 million in 2032, reflecting the issuance of more shares to fund its production growth. A successful scenario could thus result in Tamboran being worth $156 per share in 2032.

However, there is substantial risk with Tamboran given that it has no commercial production yet. It needs to cost-effectively grow production without too much further dilution.

A key factor to that will be bringing down its well costs. It is targeting a reduction to $15 million per well (from $26 million). If it can do that while also increasing its well productivity by around 10% to 20%, I think it will be in pretty good shape in terms of well economics. Higher costs would require stronger well results to offset that. Tamboran would also need to demonstrate that the Shenandoah South 6H well productivity can be matched or improved throughout more of its position too.

Thus, I would apply a discount rate of 25% per year to reflect the high risk and uncertainty around Tamboran. This would bring its current estimated value to around $41 per share.

There may be additional value from the NTLNG project, but I've left that out for now since so many details (funding, etc...) still need to get worked out for that.

Conclusion

Tamboran Resources is an interesting company that is trying to unlock a potentially massive amount of natural gas resources in Australia's Beetaloo Basin. If it is successful in reaching its goals (such as 0.8 Bcf per day in net production and $15 million well costs), it could be worth over $150 per share by 2032. I've given it a couple more years compared to its prior targets to reach those goals.

However, there is certainly a large amount of risk and uncertainty about its ability to reach those targets. While it nominally has enough cash to operate until early 2028 now, it will probably need to raise significant additional funds before it reaches positive cash flow. Thus, I've modeled its future share count at 50 million (up from 35 million, assuming the Falcon deal closes). This could easily be an underestimate if capital efficiency improvements are slower than expected.

As well, if Tamboran can't significantly reduce well costs, the overall well economics at Beetaloo may become marginal. Due to items such as that, I've assumed a significant 25% discount rate to reflect the risks to Tamboran achieving its targets. This would bring Tamboran's current value to around $41 per share.

Tamboran's estimated value depends a lot on this discount rate too. At a 20% discount rate, its estimated current value changes to $52, while at a 30% discount rate, it changes to $32.

data.title: Ur-Energy: From Survival To Execution, But Profitability Still Unproven
data.description:
It is time to update Ur-Energy (URG) as the situation changed since my last article. Also, what changed was that Seeking Alpha platform has issued a warning on the stock, and I very well agree with the rating. When I first covered the stock the company was unpredictable, unreliable and looked weak. The problems then were huge losses, derivative position which was causing a risk and most importantly. And also, let's add that I was not sure then whether the company will require new capital. The situation is quite different as of today, but still risky. Production is ramping up, Lost Creek is not an experiment now but a working asset. Balance is also stronger now with solid cash reserve. In other words, survival risk decreased. But now new questions arise whether these operations will become profitable.

What I was expecting in my last article happened. In 2025 production reached around 410 thousand pounds, which is practically similar to previously seen ramp up. Inventory also increased to 406 thousand pounds. This to me means, that the company is not only producing, but also has flexibility when fulfilling contracts and planning in advance. Compared to 2024 costs have decreased and the margins I think will soon increase. Contracted prices are stable, but importantly is that gross profit is positive at last. However, we cannot call it profitable company, but it is a breakthrough nonetheless. The margins being positive is a good signal and important moment, even if losses below can still be seen.

Finances have changed as well. Cash position increased to over $123 million, because they have been attractive capital through convertible notes. Part of the capital also came from warrant realisation. But this is the part which I don't like. Capital attraction most of the time leads to shareholder dilution. While, yes, we can say that for now this risk has decreased, I am still sceptical.

Operations

On operational side, the biggest change was not their growth, we expected it anyway, but how it changed the structure. Lost Creek throughout 2025 went from execution risk to questionable utilisation at least for me. I am not questioning whether they can reach that 400 thousand pounds level, but can they get economic value out of it. I might sound sceptical, but inventory growth might not be that good. Yes, it gives flexibility for contracts, but also it shows, that part of the production is not monetized immediately. And cash conversion then is still not ideal. It is a subtle, but very important difference between producing and earning.

Shirley Basin situation is not that simple too. The project is nearing its start, but the market already knows that and I believe stock has been priced for that. This means, that the upside is not guaranteed. If the costs decrease to promised $24 per pound level, this could change overall margin structure. But if not, we will just get more production volume with similar problem like now.

Financials

This is what I do not like either and to be fair, I do not feel like it changed. While they are moving forward with operations, but looking at it deeper, we can see that profitability quality is still not the best. Net loss in 2025 even got bigger, which is a bad sign to me. We can say that it came from growth phase as they need more employees, higher G&A costs, projects development. In other words, I agree that they are investing into the future, but it might come at a price to current investors.

Another important factor for me is that from accounting stand point, some of the lines are becoming better. However, cash flow is not that good to say that the business can maintain itself. Even though they have a lot of cash and in short-term I do not see any pressure, real cash burn is still ongoing. In fact it was around $43.1 million in 2025. It is just hidden by stronger capital base.

Balance and capital structure which I mentioned earlier has some trade-offs for me. Instead of going through simple share issuance, they have chosen convertible debt. On one had, this also works as a good signal that the management does not want to dilute shareholders immediately. On the other had, that cash is not free. Dilution did not disappear, it just god delayed and dependent on share price.

Convertible debt decreases immediate pressure and gives a little bit of flexibility, but for investors it poses new risk. As I said, in the future, the debt will be converted and it will become ceiling at one point, which will need to be broken. I believe that current comfort that URG is trying to achieve is not solving the problem.

Costs per unit decreased, which we can say in my opinion, that operational effectiveness is indeed improving. But the problem is that "all-in" cost per sold pound. There it might be due to inventory mix, that older costs and overall structure becomes heavier. They can produce cheaper, but when selling this effect disappears. Majority of contracts are long-term, and I can say that it gives some sort of stability. However, it caps the upside when uranium spot price is going up. Meaning that the company is not fully leveraged on the market price and at the same time nor really that secure if the price goes down. This middle part, decreases volatility, just I do not like that it limits potential profitability jump.

Overall, the company is transitioning and I cannot argue against that. But that transition needs to show that the company can actually make money.

Catalysts

Current catalysts are better than they were when I first analysed the company. I strongly believe that the market bought the story and is waiting for results, simply because since my first article the stock went up 50%. I doubt that Shirley start would cause major fluctuation in share price. But I think that the market will be moved if the start is going to be clean. Meaning that we will need to see whether they reached projected flow, whether costs are really dropping and look for any technical lags.

If in following quarters we see that the volume is actually becoming a real profitability (not just gross, but near bottom line), then the sentiment could change. If not, I simply believe that the market will start doubting this whole story. Let us not forget the uranium spot price, which like any other commodity recently started fluctuating a lot. The company itself is following uranium prices in one way or another and the share price will reflect on that. Why I am saying it is a catalyst? Because I believe that uranium spot price will remain around current levels.

Risks

The biggest risk currently that I see is margin compression. Even though we already established that the costs are decreasing I doubt that the company can absorb any pressure on its margins. Small problems could lead to increase in costs, or lower realised prices level. Given that they have operational leverage, with increasing volume, the system is becoming less flexible. Meaning that problems in logistics, technology could lead to the same margin compression problem.

Capital structure is always in my articles whenever the company is constantly increasing outstanding shares count. If the share price is going to go up, I believe we will start seeing dilution and natural ceiling to the price. If not, the company will have debt hanging around. This means that the same financial leverage which today allows company to grow without new share issuance could become a limit.

Conclusion

All in all, Ur-Energy is getting better. Operations are in good shape, at least it is in my opinion. The production stabilized, projects are moving according to guidance and Lost Creek gives foundation. I mentioned a lot their financials, and liquidity question is not that critical like was. However do not be mistaken, they raised capital just in other ways that I mentioned. Profitability has not been reached, margins while improving, still not stable enough for me to call them good.

Majority of my questions from previous articles have been answered, yet I do not feel confident in calling this company a buy. Now everything depends on whether operational basis will become real and sustainable profitability and until now, I am maintaining hold rating.

data.title: 5 Best Energy Stocks That Can Outperform After The Strait Opens
data.description:
Best Energy Stocks for a Widening Crack Spread

Oil has once again reminded investors how headline-driven the energy sector can be, with price swings tied to shifting developments in the Middle East. Tensions around Iran and the Strait of Hormuz have pushed crude higher in brief surges, only to fall quickly on signs of de-escalation. In this environment, high-beta upstream exploration and production (E&P) stocks have moved in close correlation with oil prices, which magnifies both upside and downside movements. However, a more attractive opportunity may lie further downstream.

Refiners (CRAK) Outperform Energy Sector (XLE) as Oil Falls From Highs

Refiners profit from the "crack spread," or the difference between the price of crude oil and the refined products, such as gasoline and diesel, produced from it. When oil prices peak and begin to stabilize or decline, refined product prices often remain relatively firm, allowing margins to expand. Therefore, refiners can outperform even after oil prices have rolled over, making them well worth a look amid the ongoing geopolitical headline noise. Seasonal gasoline demand, which rises as summer travel activity increases, can also reinforce our refiner stock thesis, adding a potential tailwind to prices.

According to the EIA (U.S. Energy Information Administration), the price of gas at the pump tends to peak in late summer. Therefore, even if de-escalation in Iran can bring oil prices down further, the price of gas will likely remain elevated for months.

5 Top Energy Stocks to Buy Now: Quant Strong Buys

To select the five best energy stocks to feature in this article, I used the Seeking Alpha Stock Screener and chose the pre-selected "Top Energy Stocks" list to review the highest Quant-Rated Strong Buy stocks. To narrow the search, I sorted by industry to target Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing, as well as the Integrated Oil and Gas Industries:

1. TotalEnergies SE (TTE)

  • Market Capitalization: $199.85B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 4 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 3 out of 16

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Integrated Oil and Gas

TotalEnergies capitalized on wartime disruption to make more than a $1B profit, purchasing nearly every available May-loading crude cargo produced in the United Arab Emirates and Oman during March, according to a Financial Times report. TTE is a global integrated energy company with operations spanning upstream oil and gas, refining, chemicals, and a growing LNG (liquefied natural gas) business. TTE’s diversified structure allows it to benefit from both downstream margin strength and rising demand for natural gas, especially as supply disruptions may continue long after the Iran conflict calms down. Therefore, as oil prices peak and volatility eases, TTE’s refining segment can support earnings, while LNG provides another growth driver, not only from constrained supply challenges but also from rising global electricity demand, including AI data center growth. From a Quant perspective, TTE’s Valuation and Profitability Factor grades are standouts.

TTE’s forward P/E offers a healthy discount of more than 37%, helping to support more room to run for the company’s price. Its growth-adjusted valuation metric, the forward PEG, also reveals an attractive discount, but even deeper at about 46%. A common thread tying together all five of my featured energy stocks, TTE’s Cash From Operations tells a story of fundamental strength, as its 27.34B CFO is 4,730% ahead of the sector median.

While the company’s profit trajectory looks to continue, TTE has not completely escaped Iran war damage, as its SATORP refinery complex, which it owns jointly with Saudi Aramco (ARMCO), has been temporarily shut down after sustaining damage last week in the Iran war. However, it’s also taken advantage of its strong position in the Middle East, as pointed out previously on its strategic purchase of oil in March. As the war deescalates, TTE demonstrates a return to performance momentum in a moderating oil environment, leading to a stock with a stronger refining profile.

2. Valero Energy Corporation (VLO)

  • Market Capitalization: $72.37B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 16 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 2 out of 18

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

Former Alpha Picks stock, which helped fuel the portfolio, Valero Energy is one of the largest independent petroleum refiners in the world. Based in the U.S., VLO operates refineries across North America and the U.K. The company is a direct beneficiary of widening crack spreads as it converts oil into gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel. In an environment where oil prices spike and retreat on headlines, VLO can outperform as refining margins widen when oil stabilizes or declines, benefiting once again from the high demand and supply chain disruptions like it saw amid the conflict between Russia and Ukraine when it was added to the Alpha Picks portfolio in May 2022. The summer travel season further supports demand for gas and jet fuel, further supporting what's already an attractive Quant perspective.

While VLO’s 'D' Valuation Grade appears to be its only weak Quant Factor, key metrics reveal a different story, especially when considering its forward growth potential. For example, VLO’s growth-adjusted valuation, the forward PEG, is attractively low at 0.44, which is nearly a 65% discount to the sector median. This leads to the company’s solid growth metrics, which have the potential to improve in the coming quarter if VLO can capitalize on a widening crack spread.

Growth metrics that support VLO’s solid growth scorecard include its forward EBITDA Growth of nearly 12%, which outpaces the sector median by more than 121%, and its long-term growth durability, as measured by the EPS Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) of more than 26%. This metric is close to 180% of VLO’s sector peers. With strong growth providing healthy free cash flow, Valero is well positioned to lead the sector even after oil peaks, and this transitions to another refiner with added midstream stability.

3. Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC)

  • Market Capitalization: $65.56B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 42 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 5 out of 18

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

Marathon Petroleum is a leading U.S. refiner with approximately 3 million barrels per day of crude oil capacity across 13 refineries, with exposure to midstream operations through its MPLX (MPLX) subsidiary. This combination can enhance earnings strength, especially as refining margins widen when crude input costs ease. Like VLO, MPC was an Alpha Picks stock that returned double-digit gains to the portfolio and stands to benefit if seasonal demand for gas and jet fuel is strong during peak travel months. Also similar to our first featured energy stock, MPC still has room to run in price, based on key valuation metrics, growth potential, and sector-leading cash flow.

MPC’s valuation/growth combination is nearly identical to VLO’s, beginning with the attractive forward PEG valuation metric of 0.44, revealing a growth-adjusted 65% discount to the sector. While Marathon also has a solid growth grade (B+) like VLO, I’m highlighting profitability here for MPC to demonstrate a few key metrics important to refiners.

An under-appreciated but highly relevant profitability metric is the Asset Turnover Ratio, which is 1.64x for MPC and nearly 282% ahead of sector peers. This tells investors that MPC is good at moving large volumes, generating revenue, and utilizing its assets. This can indicate the ability to maximize output when crack spreads are strong. The more important profitability metric, Cash From Operations, is 8.25B, over 14x bigger than the sector median, confirming its margins are real. As oil price swings calm down and gas demand remains strong, Marathon offers a balanced way to take advantage of downstream strength, leading into a smaller, mid-cap refiner with greater margin growth potential.

4. Par Pacific Holdings, Inc. (PARR)

  • Market Capitalization: $3.26B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 13 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 1 out of 18

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing

One of my current Alpha Picks holdings, Parr Pacific, is a mid-cap refining and logistics company with operations in niche markets, including Hawaii and the U.S. West Coast. The company’s geographic location allows it to benefit from larger refining margins when regional supply tightens, making it a high-beta play on crack spread widening. PARR’s impressive growth metrics and sector-leading momentum make it a compelling energy stock to hold in the current environment.

PARR's impressive forward 'A+' Growth Factor grade helps to explain how it is a top Quant-rated stock in the energy sector and number one in the Oil and Gas Refining and Marketing industry. Its forward EBITDA growth of 36% is more than 6x the sector median, while forward Operating Cash Flow Growth is above 97%, which is about 14x its sector peers. In an environment where oil prices stabilize or decline while gas and jet fuel demand rise seasonally, Par Pacific is well positioned to continue outperforming its larger sector peers (XLE).

PARR’s growth and momentum make it a good complement to larger refiners, which leads to an integrated energy stock with global exposure.

5. Repsol, S.A. (REPYY)

  • Market Capitalization: $28.16B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 11 out of 226

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 04/14/2026): 7 out of 16

  • Sector: Energy

  • Industry: Integrated Oil and Gas

Repsol is an integrated energy company based in Spain with exposure to refining, upstream production, and a growing low-carbon energy segment. Its refining business plays a central role in the current environment, where strong seasonal demand for gasoline and diesel supports margins even as crude prices fluctuate. Repsol’s investments in renewable energy also provide long-term growth potential as global energy demand evolves post-Iran conflict.

Beginning with valuation, REPYY’s forward 5.43 P/E is at a steep discount to the sector median’s 13.40, and the forward PEG of 0.30 is even steeper at more than 75%. Digging deeper, Repsol’s attractive forward EV/Sales and EV/EBITDA suggest the market is undervaluing its earnings power and cash flow potential, which leads to key profitability metrics.

As you may detect from REPYY's 'B+' Profitability Grade and seemingly average to slightly-below-average margins, the key metrics contributing to its higher overall score are Cash From Operations growth. The CFO growth of 6.30B is over 1,000% higher than the sector median, and this translates into Free Cash Flow, a core metric for fundamental strength. With a balanced mix of upstream and downstream operations, Repsol rounds out a group of companies well positioned for outperformance, which segues nicely into our conclusion.

Conclusion: Crack Spreads, LNG Exposure Can Outperform

Energy markets remain volatile, but sector opportunities are shifting downstream as refining margins may soon begin to drive performance rather than oil prices. Companies with strong exposure to crack spreads, supported by gas and jet fuel demand through the coming travel season, appear well positioned to outperform even as oil stabilizes or declines. At the same time, integrated companies with LNG exposure can provide additional balance through demands tied to global energy needs, as well as AI-related growth. Together, these top energy stocks offer a solid mix of cash flow strength, earnings potential, and upside momentum, giving investors a means of navigating headline noise and a post-war energy environment.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using Stock Screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Subscribe to Premium for unlimited access to Seeking Alpha quant ratings and expert content. Alternatively, Alpha Picks might be ideal if you're interested in two monthly stock picks of the top "strong buy" Quant stocks. Feel free to check out our new Alpha Picks app in the Apple App Store! Seeking Alpha's quant ratings and investment research tools help to ensure you are furnished with the best resources to make informed investment decisions while taking the emotion out of investing.

Happy investing!
