data.title: Goldman Sachs Q1 Results Flag Risks From Middle East Conflict And Inflation
data.description:
The market’s reaction to Goldman Sachs’ (GS) first quarter (Q1) earnings for FY2026 – made on the 13th of April 2026 – was striking: despite early trends that the company will repeat the results of Fiscal Year 2025 wherein net earnings registered strong growth over the previous year’s, the stock went on to fall by 2%.

There are two specific factors that could be attributed to this immediate conviction – one for the industry that the company operates in and one more generalized for the economy.

Trend Drilldown

In recent years, Goldman Sachs has been working on transitioning out of the consumer banking business altogether. Early in January, the company announced that it will be transitioning its Apple Card business – which represented the first consumer credit card by Goldman Sachs – to Chase over a period of 2 years. In 2025, it had entirely exited the General Motors card business as well.

Meanwhile, some line items have been merging as the company streamlines its business. For example, “Equity Investments” and “Debt Investments” are now reported in aggregate as the bank transitions from direct investments on its balance sheet to a scaled third-party funds-driven business. Previously, the bank would use its own balance sheet to make massive investments (known as “Principal Investments”) into opportunities. Going forward, it would increasingly raise money from outside investors and manage the investment in exchange for “Management Fees” and “Incentive Fees”. The line item merge in this instance is a signal of commitment to the market.

While the changes don’t alter the bottom line (i.e. earnings) or the top line (i.e. revenue) in past quarters, it does alter the horizon of the trend line analysis relative to more recent data.

One potentially significant standout in trends is in the company’s steadfast commitment to compensation and benefits trending in tandem with top- and bottom-line changes. This is a tacit recognition that banks are less about technology or infrastructure and more about the people, be they an analyst in the operations team or a high-profile wealth advisor. When the bank does well – as a general rule – those within do well and effort will be expended to retain the best among them.

In terms of revenue share, key business segments and line items are seemingly a mixed bag, albeit with a positive overall tone:

A strong pipeline in completed mergers & acquisitions (M&A) volume along with higher revenues in prime financing and “intermediation” – making markets, executing orders, and clearing transactions for clients –led the “Equities” segment within the Global Banking and Markets (GBM) division to rise 31% relative to Q1 of 2025. The Asset and Wealth Management (AWM) division was up 10% over Q1 2025 on the back of strong revenues from management and other fees.

Within GBM, the “Fixed Income, Currency and Commodities” (FICC) segment showed a 10% decline relative to Q1 2025 due to significantly lower net revenues in interest rate products, mortgages and credit products. Meanwhile, AWM’s “Private Banking and Lending” segment – which provides loans to wealth management clients against commercial and residential real estate, securities or other assets while raising deposits through said clients and Marcus – registered 12% lower than in Q1 2025. Amidst an overall positive picture for the company, these fact patterns stood out amidst market sentiment on numerous indicators.

Credit “Cockroaches” and Sticky Inflation

In October 2025, JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon created a stir during his company's earnings call when he flagged the implosion of auto lender Tricolor Holdings and car-parts supplier First Brands Group as a crisis event for private credit. “I probably shouldn’t say this, but when you see one cockroach, there are probably more”, he told analysts on the call. “Everyone should be forewarned on this one.”

The term “cockroaches” rapidly went on to become a term employed across numerous media releases by the financial services industry. In Q1 2025, Jamie Dimon’s warning wasn’t exactly unfounded: private credit markets faced a record surge in redemption requests totalling over $20 billion, up 183% from Q4 2025.

Major firms, including Blue Owl, BlackRock and Carlyle, capped withdrawals at 5% to manage liquidity, leaving roughly $7 billion in requests unmet. This highlights the increasing strain on the $2 trillion sector – which has a network of complex linkages with global investment banks.

In terms of size of redemption requests, Goldman Sachs was the largest fund manager that honoured all redemption requests, which were a little under 5% of the fund’s value. In related filings within the SEC’s Edgar system, Goldman Sachs’ Private Credit platform stated that while it did see a 7% decline in overall quarterly inflows into its private credit fund in Q4 2025, this was a more moderate decrease relative to most of their peers. Furthermore, December inflows were 11% higher than its YTD ("Year-Till-Date") average, the Q4 2025 redemption rate of 3.5% was lower than their peers who averaged above 5% and the ratio of inflows to redemptions was 3.3x. The company asserts that “uncertain markets provoke a flight to quality in investors” and that February 2026 was its platform’s second-highest subscription month since its inception.

In Conclusion

Goldman Sachs’ provision for credit losses rose nearly 10% from a year earlier to $315 million, marking its largest increase in loan loss provisions since 2020. During the earnings call, CEO David Solomon stated that while the environment for mergers and other deals has been resilient, the war in the Middle East is a point of concern to watch for. The resulting high prices of commodities could future capital markets deals like mergers or debt issuance while inflation trends will impact numerous other parts of the market that the company operates in. Solomon also said that market churn from the war had cooled IPO listings in March in particular.

As the earnings performance indicates, the company has diverse pools of revenue generation which can act as offsets or multipliers. Inflation – which has remained sticky particularly in the U.S. – is a potential risk for the company going forward as it generates 60% of its revenue from the Americas. If inflation stays elevated, consumption is impaired. Furthermore, the Fed’s rate is likely to stay “higher for longer”, which makes financing deals more expensive and the servicing of debt more burdensome. Thus, inflation becomes something of a “two-headed” beast for the likes of U.S.’ top global banks.

If inflation remains sticky and the Fed rate stays “higher for longer”, the recessionary outlook strengthens. This is when financial services stocks typically attract higher interest. While banks do get hit by recessionary effects, their historied swift and surgical approach to limiting costs and pivoting towards less risky activities – which having a diversified pool of revenues helps in doing – typically mean that they are affected less and recover faster. On top of that, Goldman Sachs also pays dividends, which tend to be another plus of investors. Goldman’s board declared that a quarterly dividend of $4.50 will paid out towards the end of June.

All in all, while markets are reacting to the potential gloom in the macro environment painted in the picture presented by the bank, the market’s immediate reaction was certainly premature; the market went on to add 2.11% to the stock’s price on the 14th of April – potentially in recognition of both the diversified revenue base and the value of holding on the stock on basis of the aforementioned merits. Nonetheless, it bears noting that no stock exists in a bubble. if markets turn bearish, Goldman Sachs’ stock would likely see volatility.

data.title: V.F. Corporation: A Decent Job Done, But I'm Not Excited Yet
data.description:
It’s been well over two years since the last time I wrote about V.F. Corporation (VFC). I said in that article that the new CEO has a lot of work to do. Since assigning the company a hold rating, the share price has appreciated by only around 15.6%, whereas if you were to buy the broader index like SPY (SPY), you’d be up over 44% on your investment. At first glance, it appears that the CEO hasn’t done anything for the shareholders thus far. Let’s dig into the overall performance of the company.

Performance

Let’s start from the top. Clearly, there has been some sort of trouble regarding its top-line performance, as its revenues fell from $11.33B to $9.58B. The management attributed the weakness to underperforming brands like Vans and Dickies, specifically calling them out as drags on performance. In some quarters over the last three years or so, these two brands experienced double-digit declines in sales, with Vans being much more of a problem since it is a much bigger brand than Dickies. Also, in the latest quarters, we can see that the company started to see some sort of recovery in sales, suggesting we may have seen the bottom.

In terms of positives, The North Face brand was the most consistent performer throughout. In the latest quarter, the brand continued to see growth of around 8%, while Vans continued to see sales declines, although it wasn’t as bad as before, with a refresh in products and digital performance improvements. And now with the sale of Dickies to Bluestar Alliance, the company has gotten rid of one of the bad-performing brands. Regionally, the Americas showed the strongest performance in the last three years.

Let’s go over VFC’s profitability in the last three years. Here, we can see that when the then-new CEO, Bracken Darrell, took over the leadership, he got a company that was kind of in shambles. Margins were contracting significantly in the first year that he took over; however, it seems that whatever he did in mid-2024 began to work as profitability reversed course, and in the latest quarters, VFC became a profitable company once again. Within the first year and a half, he went to work with the “Reinvent” transformation program, targeting at least $300m in gross cost savings by FY25, and $500-$600m of net operating income expansion by the end. I think this was quite a classic restructuring strategy that new CEOs tend to do. Clean up the portfolio, fix the pricing/channel mix, and focus on a tighter cost discipline. So far, it looks like it paid off.

We can see the same recovery in the company’s other key profitability and efficiency metrics. Although they are still not great, the uptrend gives me hope that the company will see much better returns in the long run. I’d like to see returns going back to double-digits, as they were last seen back in the beginning of 2019.

With the reinvent program, the CEO said he would like to get the company’s leverage down to 2.5x or below, so let’s take a look at the financial position in the latest quarter. VFC finished Q3 ’26 with around $1.466B in cash and equivalents, against $3.56B in long-term debt. The company is still on the hook for a lot of debt, but its cash position is also quite strong. Its operating income improved from last year, while the debt slightly came down as well, leading to an interest coverage ratio of around 7x, which is quite healthy. For reference, many analysts believe that an interest coverage ratio of 2x is healthy. I like to see at least 5x because I’m more conservative like that, and even then, the company is much stronger. So, I don’t think the leverage is an issue. Some investors hate debt overall, so I believe the management will continue to work on it. At the start of the year, the company’s leverage ratio was 4.1x, and now they are guiding to bring it down to 3.5x or below by the end of FY26, so there is still a way to go.

Overall, it seems that there has been some progress regarding the company’s progress under the new CEO, who is not so new any longer. He has been quite proactive when it comes to cutting out the fat, streamlining operations, and focusing on what will be the big drivers of performance going forward.

Comments on the Outlook

So, what are the main drivers that will continue to help VFC improve going forward? I like that Bracken Darrell is focusing on what is important in terms of brands. The two best-performing brands remain The North Face and Timberland. They are seen as the growth and profit engines for 2026, and most likely beyond. These brands have been growing mid-to high-single digits, and being of higher quality, demanding higher prices, carry much higher margins. The mix shit needs to continue towards these brands.

I’ve always seen DTC as a very attractive model, so the management paying attention to how this progresses will also be vital to improving operations in the future. The company already said that DTC “re-inflected” growth, so the management will continue to nurture the model to achieve higher margins and profitability overall.

The big elephant in the room remains the Vans brand. The management needs to clean out the shop and execute another multi-year brand reset, which would position the brand at the forefront of innovation in footwear. It doesn’t have to be too experimental in terms of design. Just introduce a cleaner product that would attract the younger generations, as it did when I was wearing them 15 years ago. It seems the brand lost its relevance and is in dire need of a reset. The brand dropped the ball and stopped innovating, and focused on a handful of archival icons like Old Skool and checkerboard slip-ons. It forgot to innovate, and that is why it started to lag behind the competitors.

The management said that stabilization of Vans will be one of the biggest swing factors for medium-term growth, but execution is going to be important here. The brand lost its cool factor, and that is sad.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at a quick valuation model to see what the company might be worth. Below are the assumptions I used.

For revenue growth, analysts expect a lot of variance, so to keep it simple, I went with quite a low growth of 3% for the next 5 years, which seems achievable. For profitability, I am assuming the company will see improvements as price mix shifts towards the better-performing brands like The North Face and Timberland. For the rest of the model, I went with the company’s historical averages. I also went with the company’s WACC of 9% as my discount rate, so there is no margin of safety, and 2.5% terminal growth rate. So, with these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we see that VFC’s fair value is around $17.67, which means it is still trading at a premium to its fair value.

Closing Comments

In the last year, the company’s share price has increased by around 68%, outperforming the SPY. Clearly, the shareholders loved the CEO’s effort to turn the company around. I just don’t think I am going to invest in it right now, because the growth is still relatively weak, and the Vans brand continues to affect the overall performance. The debt levels are still high, but sustainable, and I like what the management has done with the company as well. It just doesn’t excite me all that much.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Your Tech Sector Rorschach Test
data.description:
By Mike Larson

You’ve heard of a Rorschach test, right? The one where you’re shown a series of inkblot images - and your answers are used as a psychological analysis tool? Well today, it’s time for your tech sector test!

Here’s my MoneyShow Chart of the Day - a nine-month chart of the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ). Do you think it’s bullish? Or bearish?

Invesco QQQ Trust

(Source: StockCharts)

If you’re a bull, you’ll probably point to the recent 10-day winning streak and the fact the QQQs are back to roughly breakeven on the year. If you’re a bear, you’ll probably highlight how the QQQs have gone nowhere since November, and that we’ve been rallying on steadily falling volume.

I didn’t show the charts here. But bulls could also point to the fact the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) is near its old highs... while bears could say the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) and SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average ETF (DIA) remain well below their pre-war peaks.

So, what do you see? Something to get excited about? Or a market that still hasn’t proved itself?

Make sure you can answer that question to your own satisfaction before committing more capital here. From where I sit though, it looks like the jury is still out.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: ENOR: Unsurprising Beneficiary Of The Iran War
data.description:
The iShares MSCI Norway ETF (ENOR) shows how well Norway is placed with its pretty heavy economic skew towards select industries given the outbreak of the Iran War and the energy crisis at its route. Norway has three claims to fame:

  • Salmon
  • Oil (primary)
  • Missiles

Relative to the energy crisis, salmon isn't particularly impressive. While technically a consumer staple, it is a commodity that is subject to price changes and value changes of these companies' biological reserves, and as a superior protein can be downtraded in an inflationary environment.

What impresses about ENOR and Norway is that it's so heavy on oil E&P but also oil services, stocks which have all seen major demand given the Iran War and stranded supply risks for the Gulf states. With the ceasefire talks falling apart for the moment and a blockade in place, the conflict has not made progress to resolution other than speculation that a second round of talks might occur and that Iran is more responsive now. Moreover, Kongsberg (KBGGY) is an important exposure that continues to gain traction with the European defense spending inflection but also from the US, being an F-35 partner among other things. Greater conflict risk supports the equity.

We also think the insurance exposures as part of financials are benefiting from yield curve dynamics. Banks are more iffy. Financials are the second largest allocation in ENOR. The NOK has held on despite its risk-on character thanks to oil fundamentals.

But reinflation in other markets with less marginal currencies may eventually sideline the NOK. Moreover, the war as the principal factor leading to the positive ENOR performance may unwind, and this would not be the optimal time for the ETF as it is correlated with spot prices and E&P and oil services. It's a sensitive bet now that it's already run-up and so dependent on imminent headlines around the war.

ENOR Breakdown

Sectorally, energy sits at the top with exposures like Equinor (EQNR) and to a lesser extent Aker BP (AKRBF). While technically a materials play, they have exposure to fertiliser through Yara (YARIY) which is also correlated to supply of petroleum product and has shot up in the current crisis.

Other than that, financial exposures are considerable, with just DNB (DNBBF) at over 11%, a stock we've covered in the past and have always liked. There are also insurance exposures like Storebrand (SREDF) and Gjensidige (GJNSF).

Industrials is driven primarily by Kongsberg, the defense and missile exposure.

Consumer staples are consumer products companies like Orkla and also salmon farmers.

We can discuss each of these sectors in the bull and bear case for the ETF.

Bull Case

  • The ceasefire talks have fallen through, and the blockade could increase conflict risk and has already lead to aborted transits. Oil supply could fall once again after a breather in the weekend. This is good especially for oil E&P which is indexed towards spot prices, with Norwegian capacity entirely unhindered by the conflict.
  • Financial exposures are benefiting from yield curve dynamics especially the insurers. Higher short term rates and longer term rates will allow for duration lengthening to drive higher reserve portfolio returns, and short term rollovers will be yielding at higher spots.
  • Defense stocks should be seen positively in situations where there is more conflict.
  • Consumer staples are not the worst consumer exposure to have, and are less discretionary.

Bear Case

  • If the blockade is successful, it could spur new talks that could lead to a renewed possibility of diplomatically ending the conflict. An end to the war would assuage oil markets and cost E&P in terms of lower spot prices.
  • While insurers are alright in the current environment, banks have to deal with possibly more reserving requirements and also a flattening of the yield curve. Short term rate expectations are those that are rising, and therefore could put upward pressure on funding costs without as much benefit to returns on longer-term lending. This is a consequence of the general reinflation from higher prices at the pump as well as associated wage-price spiral effects. Moreover, with higher rates coming in for more attractive currency pairs as well, the NOK has a tendency to get sidelined for less marginal yielding currencies and we think that could materialise if reinflation persists making the previously stickier Norwegian inflation less unique as inflation becomes reintroduced everywhere. If the war ends the NOK may even do alright, as it has a risk-on character more than being correlated to fundamentals of oil according to academic research.
  • Consumer staples, particularly salmon, may be subject to downtrading in the event of inflation led demand destruction in favour of inferior proteins.

Bottom Line

The case is a little mixed. Financials are seeing a flattening yield curve but also better return dynamics for the reserve portfolios given the war and assuming it lasts. Oil is clearly benefiting from the energy crisis, as well as defense from the war risks globally. On balance, the most significant exposure being energy positions ENOR negatively if the war ends soon. If it doesn't, profits will be considerable for E&P and the conditions will start to become evident for the oil services stocks to be able to get new mandates driven by downstream CAPEX. Persistent global reinflation prevents the stickier inflation of Norway from getting the NOK's yield to stand out and also brings a risk-off attitude that the NOK can be sensitive to. The end of an energy crisis could mean the NOK goes back to standing out a bit as a high yield developed market currency. It could go any way at this point, depends on the war.

data.title: Guardian Pharmacy Services: Waiting For A Better Entry Point
data.description:
Investment summary

My recommendation for Guardian Pharmacy Services (GRDN) is a hold rating. I like the business. GRDN has a decent moat, and the IRA headwind appears manageable. The problem is valuation. A lot of the good news already looks priced in, and I do not think the current multiple leaves enough room for upside. For now, I would wait for a better entry point.

Business Overview

GRDN is a US-based long-term care pharmacy services provider. It operates a network of local pharmacies across 38 states and serves residents of assisted living facilities, behavioral health facilities, and skilled nursing facilities. The business model focuses on dispensing and delivering medications to long-term care residents and providing related services such as clinical consulting, compliance packaging, billing and administrative support, etc.

Guardian is in the right part of long-term care

I believe the most important aspect of GRDN’s equity story is that more than two-thirds of its revenue comes from assisted living facilities and behavioral health facilities, not skilled nursing. I call this out because assisted living is a more difficult operating environment for pharmacy providers. In assisted living, residents often have the right to choose their own pharmacy, unlike skilled nursing, where the facility typically uses a single provider. At the same time, residents are older, take many medications, and live in facilities with limited clinical staff. This makes medication coordination harder, and this gap creates room for a specialized operator to come in (like GRDN). We know that this is a problem to be solved because GRDN is now serving more than 205,000 residents across approximately 8,200 facilities through 54 pharmacies, with approximately 140,000 of those residents in assisted living alone. Notably, there is a 90% adoption rate in partnered assisted living facilities.

But the point is not that GRDN has size; the point is that it has achieved significant scale in this niche market. In my view, the operating landscape is split two ways: (1) skilled nursing is more mature, more contract-driven, and more constrained; and (2) assisted living, in contrast, is less standardized and gives pharmacy providers (like GRDN) more room to differentiate themselves (be it through service, coordination, execution, etc.). In the context for GRDN, this means it is not relying on overall market growth to grow. It is operating in a market where better execution can translate into more facility wins, higher adoption, and deeper relationships over time. In other words, this is a market where GRDN can reinvest aggressively to grow faster because assisted living is still a setting where customers will choose the provider that solves the most problems.

The moat

GRDN’s moat is not just distribution. It is the fact that GRDN is built into how assisted living facilities actually run (i.e., GRDN is embedded into the daily workflow). Over the years, GRDN has built tools around workflows (e.g., cost control, medication management, clinical oversight, etc.), with its most famous tools such as Guardian Compass, which helps with planning, purchasing, and operational control; and GuardianShield, which helps to garner insights from clients' data. Aside from that, you also have plenty of other tools like the Insurance Optimizer Program, the Antibiotic Stewardship Program, the Psychotropic Medication Reduction Program, etc. I do not think it is necessary to go through every tool, but the Insurance Optimizer Program is worth focusing on because it shows why GRDN is harder to replace.

Based on my understanding, the Insurance Optimizer Program works in two steps. First, it helps residents choose the right pharmacy benefit plan and navigate annual enrollment. Second, once prescriptions are being filled, GRDN works with physicians and insurance payors to get non-covered drugs approved, resolve rejected claims, and, when needed, find an alternative that is covered. Both residents and facilities would also receive reports to show how much they have saved. This may seem simple, but it serves a great value proposition to both residents and facilities. The residents want it for a simple reason: lower out-of-pocket cost and less hassle. For facilities, the value is enjoying a lower administrative burden, fewer billing-related issues to deal with, and a simpler medication process for staff to manage. For context, in 2025, this program saved residents >$56 million, and since it is working nicely, facilities are unlikely to switch, and that is a moat I like to see.

IRA is a headwind, but not a dealbreaker

The main pushback on GRDN is the change to the Inflation Reduction Act [IRA], which affects drug pricing economics. This is a real risk, and the revenue hit is real. But I don’t think investors should be super worried. While the expected 2026 impact is ~$150 million of revenue and ~$5 million to $7 million of EBITDA, management still kept 2026 revenue guidance at $1.4 billion to $1.42 billion and still raised adj. EBITDA guidance to $120 million to $124 million. Notice that while revenue got hit, profit guidance actually went up, so that tells us GRDN's underlying economics didn’t really see a big impact. In fact, it tells us that GRDN has levers it can pull to drive up margins. For instance, GRDN has already built direct relationships with payer partners and has been in discussions with Pharmacy Benefit Managers [PBMs]. This should translate to better economics since this stronger direct relationship can give GRDN a better chance to explain its value provided to residents, facilities, and health plans. So, while the IRA is clearly a headwind, it is not a dealbreaker. In my view, the more important takeaway is that GRDN’s business model is resilient enough to absorb the reset and still grow profit.

There is also a competitive angle to this. This IRA rule change that hurts the sector does not hit every operator the same way. Smaller independents have fewer tools, less negotiating power, and less room to absorb this hit. GRDN, while being impacted, really isn’t going to feel much of a big pain after this reset. The moat is still strong, and it still has scale. If anything, this puts GRDN in a better position to win market share as smaller players exit the market once their unit economics turn less attractive.

Valuation

The only problem with GRDN is valuation isn’t cheap. The stock trades at ~30x NTM PE with ~10% revenue growth CAGR once we get past this IRA reset. It is not that attractive when we compare it to Brightspring Health Services (BTSG), which trades at 27x NTM PE but has a mid-teens revenue growth outlook. Even if we adjust for the fact that GRDN has a higher margin profile vs. BTSG, I think that is already well reflected in the ~3x multiple premium. On a historical basis, GRDN is also trading at the higher end of its valuation range (average is 26x), so I am not confident that multiples will see any more re-rating from here. As such, I prefer to wait for a cheaper multiple before I can give this a buy rating.

Risk

GRDN’s workflow-driven moat may be less sticky than I thought. If tools like Insurance Optimizer, GuardianShield, and related clinical programs stop delivering clear savings, facilities may become less willing to push adoption higher. Another risk is execution from scaling. This is not a simple distribution business. Any compliance issue or medication error could damage customer trust quickly and structurally impair GRDN’s growth profile.

Conclusion

Fundamentally, I view GRDN as a high-quality operator in a niche where execution matters. It has achieved scale, has a nice moat, and has the ability to protect margins through the IRA reset. I also think GRDN can keep winning share over time, especially if smaller players feel more pressure. That said, I do not think investors need to chase the stock here. The business is good, but the valuation is not cheap.
